verses.text: Now the children of Israel after their number, to wit, the chief fathers and captains of thousands and hundreds, and their officers that served the king in any matter of the courses, which came in and went out month by month throughout all the months of the year, of every course were twenty and four thousand. Over the first course for the first month was Jashobeam the son of Zabdiel: and in his course were twenty and four thousand. Of the children of Perez was the chief of all the captains of the host for the first month. And over the course of the second month was Dodai an Ahohite, and of his course was Mikloth also the ruler: in his course likewise were twenty and four thousand. The third captain of the host for the third month was Benaiah the son of Jehoiada, a chief priest: and in his course were twenty and four thousand. This is that Benaiah, who was mighty among the thirty, and above the thirty: and in his course was Ammizabad his son. The fourth captain for the fourth month was Asahel the brother of Joab, and Zebadiah his son after him: and in his course were twenty and four thousand. The fifth captain for the fifth month was Shamhuth the Izrahite: and in his course were twenty and four thousand. The sixth captain for the sixth month was Ira the son of Ikkesh the Tekoite: and in his course were twenty and four thousand. The seventh captain for the seventh month was Helez the Pelonite, of the children of Ephraim: and in his course were twenty and four thousand. The eighth captain for the eighth month was Sibbecai the Hushathite, of the Zarhites: and in his course were twenty and four thousand. The ninth captain for the ninth month was Abiezer the Anetothite, of the Benjamites: and in his course were twenty and four thousand. The tenth captain for the tenth month was Maharai the Netophathite, of the Zarhites: and in his course were twenty and four thousand. The eleventh captain for the eleventh month was Benaiah the Pirathonite, of the children of Ephraim: and in his course were twenty and four thousand. The twelfth captain for the twelfth month was Heldai the Netophathite, of Othniel: and in his course were twenty and four thousand. Guidance: Having ordered the worship of God's house, David now orders the defense of the kingdom: a standing militia of twelve courses, each of twenty-four thousand men, serving in rotation 'month by month throughout all the months of the year.' No one bore the burden alone; each course served its month and then returned to its home and fields, sharing the duty fairly across the year. The chapter is showing us the same wisdom of order and rotation in the civil and military life of the nation that David applied to the temple service. The chapter is teaching us that good government provides for the common defense in an orderly, equitable way — burdens shared, duties rotated, so that the kingdom is always guarded yet no family is overburdened. The chapter is teaching us that there is a place for diligent, organized provision in earthly affairs as well as in worship; the same God who is honoured by ordered praise is honoured by ordered, just administration of a nation's business. The chapter is teaching us that faithful service has its seasons: each course came in and went out in its appointed month, doing its duty and then yielding to the next. So in the work of God and the work of life, we serve our turn faithfully and make room for others. The chapter is teaching us, too, that the strength of a people lies not in a few but in the many ordered together — twelve courses, hundreds of thousands of men, each in his place, made Israel secure. We are taught to value good order, shared responsibility, and faithful service in our appointed season, knowing that the LORD is the God of order in every sphere of life, and that the well-arranged labour of many hands accomplishes what no one could do alone.

Furthermore over the tribes of Israel: the ruler of the Reubenites was Eliezer the son of Zichri: of the Simeonites, Shephatiah the son of Maachah: Of the Levites, Hashabiah the son of Kemuel: of the Aaronites, Zadok: Of Judah, Elihu, one of the brethren of David: of Issachar, Omri the son of Michael: Of Zebulun, Ishmaiah the son of Obadiah: of Naphtali, Jerimoth the son of Azriel: Of the children of Ephraim, Hoshea the son of Azaziah: of the half tribe of Manasseh, Joel the son of Pedaiah: Of the half tribe of Manasseh in Gilead, Iddo the son of Zechariah: of Benjamin, Jaasiel the son of Abner: Of Dan, Azareel the son of Jeroham. These were the princes of the tribes of Israel. But David took not the number of them from twenty years old and under: because the LORD had said he would increase Israel like to the stars of the heavens. Joab the son of Zeruiah began to number, but he finished not, because there fell wrath for it against Israel; neither was the number put in the account of the chronicles of king David. Guidance: David appoints rulers over each of the tribes, that the whole nation might be well led. But the Chronicler adds a deeply instructive note: 'David took not the number of them from twenty years old and under: because the LORD had said he would increase Israel like to the stars of the heavens.' And he recalls the earlier disaster — 'Joab... began to number, but he finished not, because there fell wrath for it against Israel.' The chapter is showing us a David chastened and grown wiser by his great sin in numbering the people (ch. 21). He had learned not to count Israel presumptuously, but to rest in the promise of God. The chapter is teaching us a precious lesson about trusting God's word rather than our own reckonings: the LORD had promised to make Israel as the stars of heaven, and David, remembering both the promise and the wrath that came from counting, refrained from numbering the young. He chose to lean on God's promise of increase rather than on a tally of his own. The chapter is teaching us that the memory of past chastening should make us wiser and more humble; David did not repeat his old sin, but governed himself by the painful lesson he had learned. The chapter is teaching us not to put our confidence in numbers, statistics, or visible measures of strength, but in the promises of God, who can increase His people beyond counting. The chapter is teaching us that there are some things better left in God's hands than reduced to our reckoning — that faith trusts the Promiser more than the spreadsheet. David's restraint here, born of a humbled heart, stands as a quiet testimony that he had truly learned from his fall: the man who once sinned by counting now trusts God to multiply, and refuses to number what God has promised to make as countless as the stars.

And over the king's treasures was Azmaveth the son of Adiel: and over the storehouses in the fields, in the cities, and in the villages, and in the castles, was Jehonathan the son of Uzziah: And over them that did the work of the field for tillage of the ground was Ezri the son of Chelub: And over the vineyards was Shimei the Ramathite: over the increase of the vineyards for the wine cellars was Zabdi the Shiphmite: And over the olive trees and the sycomore trees that were in the low plains was Baalhanan the Gederite: and over the cellars of oil was Joash: And over the herds that fed in Sharon was Shitrai the Sharonite: and over the herds that were in the valleys was Shaphat the son of Adlai: Over the camels also was Obil the Ishmaelite: and over the asses was Jehdeiah the Meronothite: And over the flocks was Jaziz the Hagerite. All these were the rulers of the substance which was king David's. Guidance: The Chronicler lists David's stewards — the men set over the king's treasures, storehouses, fields, vineyards, olive trees, oil cellars, herds, camels, asses, and flocks. Every kind of property had its faithful overseer, each labour and resource carefully managed. The chapter is showing us the diligent, orderly stewardship of all that God had given David; nothing was left to neglect, but each part of the king's substance was entrusted to a capable, accountable man. The chapter is teaching us the dignity and importance of faithful stewardship in ordinary, earthly things. These were not priests or prophets, but managers of fields and flocks — yet their work is recorded in Scripture with honour, for the careful tending of resources is a godly calling. The chapter is teaching us that the LORD is honoured by diligence and good management in the affairs of this life; whether we oversee treasures or tend vineyards, herds, or olive trees, faithful work done well is service that God notices and values. The chapter is teaching us not to despise the practical and the material as though only 'spiritual' work mattered; David's kingdom needed its keepers of the storehouses and its herdsmen as truly as its priests and singers, and all alike served under God. The chapter is teaching us that everything we possess is a trust to be managed faithfully — for the Christian, we are all stewards of what God has lent us, and we shall give account of our diligence. The variety of overseers — over wine and oil, over Sharon's herds and the valley's flocks, over camels and asses — teaches us that there is a place for every kind of honest skill in the service of a well-ordered life, and that faithfulness in managing the substance God gives is itself a way of glorifying Him. Let us be like these stewards: diligent, accountable, and faithful in the practical trust committed to our care.

And over the king's treasures was Azmaveth the son of Adiel: and over the storehouses in the fields, in the cities, and in the villages, and in the castles, was Jehonathan the son of Uzziah: And over them that did the work of the field for tillage of the ground was Ezri the son of Chelub: And over the vineyards was Shimei the Ramathite: over the increase of the vineyards for the wine cellars was Zabdi the Shiphmite: And over the olive trees and the sycomore trees that were in the low plains was Baalhanan the Gederite: and over the cellars of oil was Joash: And over the herds that fed in Sharon was Shitrai the Sharonite: and over the herds that were in the valleys was Shaphat the son of Adlai: Over the camels also was Obil the Ishmaelite: and over the asses was Jehdeiah the Meronothite: And over the flocks was Jaziz the Hagerite. All these were the rulers of the substance which was king David's. Guidance: The Chronicler lists David's stewards — the men set over the king's treasures, storehouses, fields, vineyards, olive trees, oil cellars, herds, camels, asses, and flocks. Every kind of property had its faithful overseer, each labour and resource carefully managed. The chapter is showing us the diligent, orderly stewardship of all that God had given David; nothing was left to neglect, but each part of the king's substance was entrusted to a capable, accountable man. The chapter is teaching us the dignity and importance of faithful stewardship in ordinary, earthly things. These were not priests or prophets, but managers of fields and flocks — yet their work is recorded in Scripture with honour, for the careful tending of resources is a godly calling. The chapter is teaching us that the LORD is honoured by diligence and good management in the affairs of this life; whether we oversee treasures or tend vineyards, herds, or olive trees, faithful work done well is service that God notices and values. The chapter is teaching us not to despise the practical and the material as though only 'spiritual' work mattered; David's kingdom needed its keepers of the storehouses and its herdsmen as truly as its priests and singers, and all alike served under God. The chapter is teaching us that everything we possess is a trust to be managed faithfully — for the Christian, we are all stewards of what God has lent us, and we shall give account of our diligence. The variety of overseers — over wine and oil, over Sharon's herds and the valley's flocks, over camels and asses — teaches us that there is a place for every kind of honest skill in the service of a well-ordered life, and that faithfulness in managing the substance God gives is itself a way of glorifying Him. Let us be like these stewards: diligent, accountable, and faithful in the practical trust committed to our care.

Also Jonathan David's uncle was a counsellor, a wise man, and a scribe: and Jehiel the son of Hachmoni was with the king's sons: And Ahithophel was the king's counsellor: and Hushai the Archite was the king's companion: And after Ahithophel was Jehoiada the son of Benaiah, and Abiathar: and the general of the king's army was Joab. Guidance: The chapter closes with David's chief counsellors and officers — Jonathan his uncle, 'a wise man, and a scribe'; Ahithophel the counsellor; Hushai 'the king's companion'; Jehoiada, Abiathar, and Joab the general of the army. David did not rule alone but surrounded himself with advisors and trusted men. The chapter is showing us the wisdom of a king who valued counsel and gathered wise men around him; even the greatest leader needs godly advisors. The chapter is teaching us the great value of wise counsel: 'in the multitude of counsellors there is safety' (Prov 11:14), and David, for all his gifts, leaned on the wisdom of others. We are taught to seek and prize good counsel, and not to trust our own judgment alone. The chapter is teaching us, too, a sobering note for those who know David's full story: Ahithophel, here named the king's counsellor, would later betray David and join Absalom's rebellion, while Hushai 'the king's companion' would remain loyal and overturn Ahithophel's counsel (2 Sam 15-17). Even among trusted advisors, one proved false and one proved faithful. The chapter is teaching us that not all who stand close to us will prove true, and that the LORD, who can turn the wisest counsel of traitors to foolishness, is the true keeper of His servants. The chapter is teaching us to value the faithful companion, the loyal friend who stays true, more than the brilliant advisor who may betray. And it is teaching us, as David's reign is thus fully ordered — worship, defense, governance, and counsel all arranged — that a well-led people is a great blessing, and that the LORD provides His servants with helpers, counsellors, and companions for the work He gives them. Let us be faithful counsellors and true companions to those we serve, and let us, above all, take counsel of the LORD, whose wisdom never fails and whose friendship never betrays.
